coredata.dc:title: Heterogeneous nucleation and synthesis of carbon dots hybrid Zr-based MOFs for simultaneous recognition and effective removal of tetracycline
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Qingfeng
Hong, Hui
Luo, Yongkang
coredata.dc:description: Discharged tetracycline (TC) seriously threatens human health and environmental safety. Herein, we developed a novel 3D flower-on-sheet structure of Zr-metal organic framework adsorbent with the assistant of N,S co-doped carbon dots as heterogeneous nucleation (UiO-67/NSC) for selective and effective removal of TC residue from contaminated water. Functional groups of UiO-67/NSC acts as a tentacle to identify TC molecules, and TC-adsorbed NSC can be separated easily by incorporating in UiO-67 structure. Hierarchical UiO-67/NSC exhibited excellent recognition performance with a wide sensitive range (0.08–20.0 mg/L) and low detection limit (0.063 mg/L) for TC. Particularly, vertically oriented 2D nanosheets in UiO-67/NSC alleviate the diffusion barrier and enhance mass transfer between adsorption sites and TC molecules, endowing it with an ability that can improve the TC adsorption capacity of parent UiO-67 to 427.35 mg/g. UiO-67/NSC with 0D structure has no significant toxic effect on human cells (HepG2 and Caco-2), which demonstrate the prepared hybrid structure is safety in practical application. Our data indicate the great potential of UiO-67/NSC as an innocuous, bifunctional, smart adsorbent for the effective detection and removal of TC residues, and we not only provide a rational design of advanced metal organic framework-based hierarchical adsorbents, but also shed light on an available method to toxic antibiotic residue recognition and water remediation.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Discharged tetracycline (TC) seriously threatens human health and environmental safety. Herein, we developed a novel 3D flower-on-sheet structure of Zr-metal organic framework adsorbent with the assistant of N,S co-doped carbon dots as heterogeneous nucleation (UiO-67/NSC) for selective and effective removal of TC residue from contaminated water. Functional groups of UiO-67/NSC acts as a tentacle to identify TC molecules, and TC-adsorbed NSC can be separated easily by incorporating in UiO-67 structure. Hierarchical UiO-67/NSC exhibited excellent recognition performance with a wide sensitive range (0.08–20.0 mg/L) and low detection limit (0.063 mg/L) for TC. Particularly, vertically oriented 2D nanosheets in UiO-67/NSC alleviate the diffusion barrier and enhance mass transfer between adsorption sites and TC molecules, endowing it with an ability that can improve the TC adsorption capacity of parent UiO-67 to 427.35 mg/g. UiO-67/NSC with 0D structure has no significant toxic effect on human cells (HepG2 and Caco-2), which demonstrate the prepared hybrid structure is safety in practical application. Our data indicate the great potential of UiO-67/NSC as an innocuous, bifunctional, smart adsorbent for the effective detection and removal of TC residues, and we not only provide a rational design of advanced metal organic framework-based hierarchical adsorbents, but also shed light on an available method to toxic antibiotic residue recognition and water remediation.

ce:section.ce:para: Antibiotics have been used in various fields to prevent or to treat microbial infectious diseases due to their antibacterial effects and low cost [1], while the abuse of antibiotics have led to many issues owing to their incomplete metabolism in humans and animals [2]. In detail, the unmetabolized antibiotics are released and lead the pollution environments, and the existing antibiotics in such environments creates potential toxicity to fish, shrimp, wheat, and other food raw materials [3]. Antibiotic residues in water and food raw materials can also accumulate in the human body through the food chain, which seriously threatens the consumers’ security [4,5]. To protect public health and control contaminants, efficient recognition and removal of antibiotics from contaminated water is in high demand.

Recently, adsorption technology has drawn remarkable attention from scientists for antibiotics removal from wastewater because of its low cost, high efficiency, and easy operation properties, and a series of advanced adsorbents with different structures was designed and applied, such as ethylenediamine tetraacetic acid derived carbon dots [6], graphene-like layered hexagonal boron nitride nanosheets [7], Zr-based UiO-66 [8,9], and biomass-based activated carbons [10]. Those adsorbents with zero-, two-, or three-dimensional (3D) nanostructure have different adsorption process and capacity for antibiotics, which is contributed to the diverse adsorption sites that are available to adsorbates inner or outer of the adsorbents [11]. Carbon dots as the typical zero-dimensional (0D) nanoadsorbents with larger specific surface area and more superficial functional groups than two-dimensional (2D) and 3D nanomaterials exhibit more affinity and shorter adsorption time towards different organic contaminants. Although the carbon dots have been employed to detect organic pollutants in water, there are rare reports on its adsorption application, which result from that its small size (<10 nm) and superior water dispersion give a critical barrier for the separation of antibiotics adsorbed carbon dots. The existence of carbon dots in adsorption systems released to environment without treatment may cause potential toxic effects on aquatic animals through entering into cells [12], which also limit its adsorption application. Besides, adsorbents including hexagonal boron nitride nanosheets with 2D nanostructure also were employed to separate adsorbates from wastewater and remediate aqueous solutions, which is due to their low mass-transfer barrier, superior anion exchange properties and high percentages of exposed active sites [13]. Nevertheless, van der Waals forces, hydrogen bond, or π-π interactions between the adjacent layers can enable the inevitable aggregation of 2D nanoadsorbents to bulk with 3D structure, which reduce surface area adhering to target molecules and hinder antibiotics adsorption efficiency, resulting in deterioration of removal performance in practical applications.

3D Metal organic frameworks (MOFs) are novel crystalline porous materials composed of metal centers/clusters and organic ligands [14]. Attributing to their diversified structures, tunable sizes, and abundant adsorption sites, 3D MOFs as designed were well applied in enrichment of hazardous trace gases [15], assessment of food safety as biosensing platforms [16], as well as adsorption of organic dyes [17]. Compared with traditional solid adsorbents, 3D MOFs exhibit great affinity, excellent adsorption performance, and higher removal efficiency towards organic pollutants benefitting from their unique binding sites, suitable particle size, and functionalized adsorption channel [18]. However, the relatively low selective recognition capability restricts MOFs’ specific applications [19], and the collapse of water-unstable MOF-based adsorbents threatens environmental safety and human health through release of toxic metal ions from metal centers or clusters [20], limiting their further application in water purification. Meanwhile, the most adsorbents cannot simultaneously recognize and remove organic pollutants in contaminated water.

Hence, to mitigate the application defects of adsorbents and improve the antibiotics adsorption efficiency in water purification, we designed and successfully fabricated a 3D hierarchical Zr-MOF based smart adsorbent with 0D N,S co-doped carbon dots (NSC) and 2D MOF sheets, UiO-67/NSC, via a simple solvothermal method for the first time. The functional smart adsorbent was systematically characterized and applied to selective recognition and removal of tetracycline (TC) as one of extensive antibiotics residues. UiO-67, an environment-friendly 3D MOF, has higher specific surface area (2300 m2 g−1), larger pores (1.17–1.61 nm), and better water stability than the others [21], making them ideal supports for the superb building platform and more appropriate candidates for TC capture. The NSC as heterogeneous nucleation sites can combine with Zr 4+ ions and then grow UiO-67 with a uniform 2D sheet morphology, which may simultaneously provide specific adsorption sites, abate mass transfer barrier, and improve the adsorption capacity of TC. Moreover, the separation efficiency of antibiotics-adsorbed NSC from contaminated systems can be promoted through separating with worked UiO-67/NSC. By making full use of abundant μ 3-OH and-NH2 groups on the surface of UiO-67/NSC and hierarchical structure, the versatile adsorbent exhibited both TC-recognition ability and adsorption capacity which is higher than that of bulk UiO-67. The probable adsorption mechanism was studied by adsorption experiments and systematically characterizations with XPS and FTIR techniques. These results suggest that adsorption capacity of TC on UiO-67 can be enhanced by converting it into 3D morphology with 0D or 2D structure, and the designed UiO-67/NSC is a promising MOF-based versatile adsorbent to simultaneously recognize and remove TC residue in the management of organic pollutants.

Zirconium chloride (ZrCl4) and biphenyl-4,4′-dicarboxylic acid (BPDC) were obtained from Shanghai Aladdin Bio-Chem Technology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). TC was purchased from Shanghai Chemical Reagent Macklin (Shanghai, China). Cell counting kit-8 (CCK-8) was provided by Beijing Solarbio Science & Technology Co., Ltd. (China, Beijing). N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF), glacial acetic acid, and acetone were supplied by Beijing Chemical Reagent Company (Beijing, China), and all other chemicals were reagent grade and used as received without further purification.

All chemicals were used as received without further purification. The UiO-67/NSC was prepared with solvothermal synthetic method. In detail, 233.0 mg of ZrCl4 was dissolved in 30 ml of DMF. Then, 2.0 ml of NSC as heterogeneous nucleation (the preparation method shown in Supplementary Information) was added to the above solution and stirred for 30 min to form a homogeneous solution. 242.2 mg of BPDC and 2.0 ml of glacial acetic acid were mixed with the homogeneous solution by bath sonication. Afterward, the as-obtained solution was heated at 120 ℃ for 24 h in a 50 ml Teflon-lined, stainless steel autoclave. When the reaction temperature turned to 25 ℃, the UiO-67/NSC composite was separated from the reaction system by centrifugation and washed with DMF and acetone several times to remove unreacted precursors. The washed UiO-67/NSC was dried further at 80 ℃ for 6 h in a vacuum. The effects of NSC contents on the shape of UiO-67/NSC were conducted by adding 0.2 ml of NSC into the synthesis system without changing of the other conditions, and the prepared adsorbents with this treatment method was named UiO-67/NSC-0.2. Parent UiO-67 was fabricated with a similar method except for the addition of NSC composites.

The surface morphologies of parent UiO-67, UiO-67/NSC-0.2, and UiO-67/NSC were characterized by a field scanning electron microscope (SEM), Carl Zeiss EVO-18 microscope, equipped with an energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) detector. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was used for collecting images of prepared UiO-67/NSC. The micro-structures of UiO-67/NSC and UiO-67 were analyzed by powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) with Cu-Kα (λ = 1.5406 Å) radiation with a scanning range of 5–80° (2θ) at a scanning rate of 5 deg/min. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra of UiO-67/NSC were recorded on an Axis Ultra DLD X-ray photo-electron spectrometer equipped with an Al Ka X-ray source (1486.6 eV) for the study of chemical components. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was used to identify the chemical bonds of UiO-67 and UiO-67/NSC on a Perkin Elmer Spectrum One instrument in the 400–4000 cm−1 range with the KBr pellet technique. Optical properties of UiO-67/NSC were studied by UV–vis adsorption spectra and fluorescence spectra at room temperature. The zeta potential of UiO-67/NSC was measured using a Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS90 under different pH conditions.

Stock UiO-67/NSC or TC solution was prepared by dissolving 100.0 mg in freshly deionized water to a concentration of 1000 mg/L and stored in a refrigerator. The excitation wavelength (λex) was changed from 260 nm to 350 nm to investigate the effect of this change on the detection system, and the detection conditions were optimized by measuring variation in fluorescence intensity of the UiO-67/NSC before and after buffer solution (2–10) or incubation time (0–30 min) changing. Under the optimized conditions, UiO-67/NSC and TC molecules were diluted with glycine-HCl acid buffer solution (pH 3) to a predetermined concentration (20 mg/L for UiO-67/NSC and 0–25.0 mg/L for TC), and the obtained mixture was added to a 2.0 ml centrifuge tube at room temperature. After incubation for 20 min, the emission spectra of UiO-67/NSC were collected from 340 to 500 nm at the excitation wavelength of 315 nm for data analysis. Moreover, the effects of the major inorganic ions Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+ Zn2+, and Cu2+ on the identification of TC were determined under the optimized conditions. The selectivity of UiO-67/NSC for sensing TC was evaluated by measuring the changes of fluorescence intensity after adding TC and the other antibiotics with the same concentrations. This approach was used to identify TC residues in real samples of contaminated water, fish muscle, and sheep muscle to evaluate its reliability.

We studied the effects of media pH (2–10), contact time (5–240 min), and initial adsorbate concentration (10–100 mg/L) on adsorption capacity of TC on UiO-67/NSC, and the initial pH of adsorption systems was adjusted by negligible volumes of 0.1 M HCl or 0.1 M NaOH. Adsorption kinetic studies were conducted by adding 20 mg/L of UiO-67/NSC or UiO-67 and 80 mg/L of TC into a 50 ml Teflon flask at pH 3, and the residual TC was measured at different time intervals (5–240 min). Adsorption isotherm experiments were obtained at pH 3 with different concentrations of TC molecules (10–100 mg/L) for 180 min under 298 K, 308 K, and 318 K. After the adsorption reaction, TC-adsorbed UiO-67/NSC or UiO-67 was separated from the adsorption systems. The supernatant was measured using a UV–vis spectrophotometer at a maximum wavelength of 357 nm, and the concentration of residual TC in adsorption systems was calculated with a standard curve formula. Regeneration ability of UiO-67/NSC were investigated by adsorption–desorption experiments. The desorption experiments were conducted by washing TC-adsorbed adsorbents with distilled water and 0.1 M NaOH solution for several times, then collected the regenerated adsorbent by centrifugation for the other cycles. The adsorption capacity of reused UiO-67/NSC was tested at room temperature. All experiments were performed three times, and the amount of adsorbed adsorbate on UiO-67/NSC or UiO-67 was calculated by Eq. (1) as follows:
(1)

where qt (mg/g) is the adsorption capacity of TC on UiO-67/NSC or UiO-67, and C i (mg/L) and C t (mg/L) are equal to the initial and supernatant concentration of TC at time t, respectively. V (L) is consistent with the volume of adsorption system, and m (g) represents the mass of UiO-67/NSC or UiO-67 in the adsorption process.

Human hepatocytes (HepG2) and intestinal epithelial cells (Caco-2) were cultured in glucose Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM). This was supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS) in a fully humidified atmosphere that contained 5% CO2 at 310 K, and the medium was refreshed every 3 d. The cytotoxic effects of UiO-67/NSC on HepG2 and Caco-2 were evaluated using a CCK-8 assay according to the manufacturer's instructions. Then, 1 × 104 cells/well were seeded in a 96-well plate and exposed to different concentrations of adsorbent (0–100 mg/L) for 24 h. Subsequently, the medium in the 96-well plate was washed and removed by phosphate-buffered saline (PBS). Then, 100 μl of the complete medium that contained 10 μl of CCK-8 solution was added into each well, and the system was incubated at 310 K for 2 h. The optical density of each sample was measured at a wavelength of 450 nm by a multimode microplate reader. A negative control (100% viable) was adopted based on the absence of UiO-67/NSC.

XPS analysis of UiO-67/NSC showed that C1s, Zr3d, O2p, N1s, and S2p of UiO-67 and NSC were observed in the survey spectrum of UiO-67/NSC (Fig. 4 a), which suggested that NSC was combined with UiO-67. These results correlated perfectly with the EDX and SEM-mapping analysis of UiO-67/NSC. In addition, the Zr3d peak at 185.0 eV was consistent with the ZrO bonds of UiO-67 [25], which revealed that the beneficial properties of UiO-67 were inherent in UiO-67/NSC. We also measured FTIR spectra to study the chemical bonds of the nanomaterials we obtained (Fig. 4b), in which we observed peaks at 662 and 769 cm−1 that were ascribed to stretching vibration and in-plane bending vibration of ZrO bonds; this is the characteristic chemical bond for UiO-67 [17]. The peaks at 1400–1680 cm−1 were attributed to the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibration of OCO groups and the CC bonds of the benzene ring, which indicated that UiO-67 persisted in the structure of the UiO-67/NSC nanocomposite. Moreover, the chemical bonds of NSC were observed in the spectrum of synthesized UiO-67/NSC. For instance, the peaks at 1016 cm−1 (CO/CN), 1180 cm−1 (CS), 1612 cm−1 (CO), 2912 cm−1 (CH), and 3420 cm−1 (OH/NH) in the FTIR spectrum for NSC were observed also in the spectrum for UiO-67/NSC [26]. However, the characteristic peak of NSC at 2056 cm−1 (terminally nitrogen-bonded NSC groups) was not observed in the prepared UiO-67/NSC nanocomposite [27,28], which suggested that the synthesis of UiO-67/NSC changed the CN bonds through the combination of NSC groups with Zr4+ ions during the solvothermal process. A new peak at 1716 cm−1 emerged in the spectrum for the UiO-67/NSC nanocomposite, which was assigned to the stretching vibration of carbonyl CO groups [29]. The other new peak at 1541 cm−1 was due to the bending vibration of NH bonds in the urethane group of the UiO-67/NSC nanocomposite [27]. These results indicated that the NSC nanocomposite was well contained in UiO-67/NSC.

The optical properties of prepared UiO-67/NSC were confirmed by UV–vis adsorption spectra and fluorescence spectra. UV–vis absorption spectra have been recorded in the wavelength range 200–800 nm for UiO-67, NSC, and UiO-67/NSC nanocomposite (Fig. 5 ). The absorbance peaks at 228 nm and 292 nm were assigned to the π–π* transition of CC bonds and to the n-π* transition of the carbonyl/amine functional groups on the surface of NSC and/or UiO-67 [30], which emerged in the spectrum for UiO-67/NSC. This indicated that most chemical bonds (CC bonds and CO bonds) of the UiO-67 and NSC existed in the novel UiO-67/NSC, and it also suggested that UiO-67/NSC was synthesized successfully by the one-pot method. The emission spectrum of UiO-67/NSC was observed at excitation wavelength λex = 315 nm and the emission wavelength λem = 410 nm. Additionally, the emission wavelength of UiO-67/NSC was different from that of UiO-67 (λem = 404 nm) under the same excitation wavelength, which probably resulted from the effect of combining NSC with UiO-67 on the luminescence of UiO-67/NSC. Notably, the fluorescence intensity decreased when TC molecules were present in the UiO-67/NSC solution, which suggested that UiO-67/NSC has great potential as a novel luminescence adsorbent in the detection of TC residues. The results further demonstrated that the precursors were integrated successfully into the UiO-67/NSC adsorbent, which can be used for sensing organic pollutants.

The emission spectra of UiO-67/NSC for identifying TC were investigated by measurement of the fluorescence intensity under different excitation wavelength conditions. The emission spectra of UiO-67/NSC were shifted to longer wavelengths with the increase in excitation wavelength (Fig. 6 a), but the fluorescence intensity increased to the maximum at 315 nm and then decreased with further increase in the excitation wavelength. Thus, the excitation wavelength λex = 315 nm was chosen for the later experiments of TC recognition. The effects of different buffers with various pH for sensing TC, such as glycine-HCl acid buffer solution, acetate buffer solution, and carbonate buffer solution, were studied. Fluorescence intensity of UiO-67/NSC before (F 0) and after (F) adding TC molecules decreased the most for glycine-HCl acid buffer at pH 3 (Fig. S6), which may be ascribed to the higher affinity of UiO-67/NSC to target molecules under that conditions. Therefore, glycine-HCl acid buffer (pH 3) was chosen as the optimal buffer system for TC detection. The fluorescence intensity of UiO-67/NSC decreased gradually with prolonged incubation time (0–15 min) (Fig. 6b), resulting from the reaction between TC molecules and the UiO-67/NSC adsorbent. With a further increase in incubation time (15–30 min), fluorescence intensity of UiO-67/NSC did not decrease significantly, which suggested that the relentless accumulation of TC molecules on the surface of UiO-67/NSC had little effect on the fluorescence intensity during that period. To ensure the stability of the fluorescence signal and the accuracy of experimental data, 15 min was used as the optimal incubation time for the following experiments.

The prepared UiO-67/NSC was employed as a sensitive smart adsorbent to detect TC, and the relationship between concentrations of TC and fluorescence intensities of UiO-67/NSC were investigated under optimal experimental conditions. Fluorescence intensity at 410 nm was strong before adding TC molecules (Fig. 7 a). However, fluorescence intensity of UiO-67/NSC decreased with the increase of TC concentration from 0.0 mg/L to 25.0 mg/L, and the corresponding linearity between the Napierian logarithm of fluorescence intensities of the UiO-67/NSC adsorbent and the different concentrations of TC was strong (Fig. 7b). The Napierian logarithm of fluorescence intensities, ln (F0/F), had a good linear relationship with the concentration of target molecules in a wide range from 0.08 to 20 mg/L with a correlation coefficient of 0.991. The limit of detection (LOD) is the concentration of TC that reduced the fluorescence of the detection system at least three times the standard deviation lower than that of the background, and it was calculated with a reported method [31]. The corresponding mathematical expression for LOD is Eq. (2):
(2)
L D
=
l n
F b
F b
- 3
S d
- b
a
where LD represents the LOD of the established TC sensing method, and Fb is the fluorescence intensity of UiO-67/NSC without the addition of TC molecules. Sd is the standard deviation for measured blank samples. a and b are the slope and intercept of the standard curve for detecting TC, respectively. Moreover, the proposed method for recognizing TC was comparable or even better than other TC sensors (Table S1), and LOD for UiO-67/NSC detection of TC was 0.063 mg/L which is lower than that of maximum residue limits established by countries or international organizations [32], which suggested that UiO-67/NSC met the requirement of TC detection for control of contaminants.

The influence of inorganic ions that exist in TC contaminated water on the TC identifying was investigated with different concentrations of NaCl, KCl, CaCl2, MgCl2 ZnSO4, and CuSO4 in the range of 0–4.0 mM under optimized conditions (Fig. S7a). The fluorescence intensity of UiO-67/NSC did not decrease significantly with the presence of monovalent ions because Na+ and K+ ions combined with TC did not inhibit the reaction between target molecules and the functional groups on the surface of the UiO-67/NSC. In addition, Ca2+, Mg2+, Zn2+ and Cu2+ ions that were added to the detection system also did not affect greatly the fluorescence intensity of UiO-67/NSC, which indicated that Ca2+, Mg2+, Zn2+ and Cu2+ did not impede the loading of TC on the smart adsorbent’s surface and blind the active sites of TC. The results implied that UiO-67/NSC was stable and reliable for the recognition of TC residue.

To evaluate further the reliability of the UiO-67/NSC sensing system, the selectivity of UiO-67/NSC for TC was studied before and after adding various antibiotics. There was no significant decrease in fluorescence intensity of UiO-67/NSC after adding 1-fold, 5-fold and 10-fold TC concentrations of sulfadiazine, oxytetracycline, or streptomycin under the same experimental conditions (Fig. S7b). However, the presence of TC molecules resulted in a dramatic decrease in fluorescence intensity of the detection system, which indicated that the UiO-67/NSC as versatile adsorbent can recognize TC molecules from different antibiotic contaminants.

Experimental conditions play a vital role in adsorption and removal of TC residues, and the effects of media pH, contact time, and TC concentration on TC adsorption were studied. As shown in Fig. S8a, adsorption capacity of TC on UiO-67/NSC increased with an increase in pH (2.0–3.0), and the maximum adsorption capacity was achieved at pH 3.0, which probably resulted from the strong affinity between UiO-67/NSC and TCH3 + rather than UiO-67/NSC and protons [33]. With an increase in pH from 3.0 to 7.0, the adsorption capacity of TC decreased slightly because the TCH3 + had changed to a new form as TCH2 ± (3.3 < pH < 7.68), which resulted in a decrease in positive charge on the surface of TC; this inhibited UiO-67/NSC and TC molecules from combining [6]. In addition, the phenolic diketone moiety in TCH2 ± structure competed with UiO-67/NSC for the H+ ions under pH 3.0–7.0, which also impeded the TC adsorption process. With a further increase in pH into the alkaline region, the adsorption capacity decreased dramatically, which resulted from the negative influence of TCH− or TC2− on the adsorption capacity. Moreover, the adsorption capacities for TC in acidic or neutral medium was higher than that in alkaline medium, which suggested that UiO-67/NSC adsorbents can be used in the environment where other unstable adsorbents cannot be applied. Adsorption capacity of TC increased with increasing contact time (Fig. S8b). The adsorption capacity increased drastically during the first 100 min, and then the TC adsorption rate slowed down, which suggested the active site on the surface of UiO-67/NSC was occupied by TC molecules. Adsorption systems reached equilibrium within 120 min after adsorption capacity no longer increased. Moreover, the concentration of adsorbate was also a critical factor for adsorption of TC. The amount of adsorbed TC increased gradually with increased concentration of TC (10–80 mg/L), and then with an increase in TC concentration > 80 mg/L adsorption capacity increased no further (Fig. S8c). Thus, 80 mg/L was chosen as the optimal TC concentration in the following experiments.

To evaluate the adsorption process and the reaction behavior of UiO-67/NSC or UiO-67 and adsorbates under same experimental conditions, our experimental data were simulated with pseudo-first order and pseudo-second order models (Fig. S8d), and the corresponding models are described as Eqs. (3) and (4), respectively.

where qe (mg/g) is the quantity of TC molecules adsorbed at equilibrium, and q t (mg/g) is the adsorption capacity of UiO-67/NSC or UiO-67 for TC at time t. k1 (min−1) and k2 (g/mg/min) stand for the rate constant of the pseudo-first order and pseudo-second order kinetic constant, respectively.

The relative kinetic parameters and coefficient of determination (R2) are listed in Table S2. The experimental data of UiO-67/NSC was fitted better by the pseudo-second order model with higher R2 (0.995) than with the pseudo-first order model, which is consistent with the results of UiO-67 groups and imply chemisorption process dominate the TC adsorption processes. The calculated adsorption capacity was well approached by the experimental data (319.22 mg/g) that is higher than that of UiO-67, suggesting the reaction between TC molecules and UiO-67/NSC was a chemisorption process [34,35], and the amount of available binding sites limited the adsorption rate and adsorption capacity. These results reveal UiO-67/CNS with 2D nanosheets can significantly promote the TC adsorption capacity of bulk UiO-67.

The intraparticle diffusion model was also used to clarify the adsorption mechanism and adsorption process of TC on UiO-67/NSC or UiO-67, and the mathematical expression of the model is presented by the following Eq. (5):
(5)
q t
=
k i
t
0.5
+ C
where q t (mg/g) stands for the amount of TC molecules adsorbed on UiO-67/NSC or UiO-67 at time t, and ki (mg/g/min) is the intraparticle diffusion rate constant. C is the intercept, which is determined by the plot of q t vs t 0.5 (Table S2). The linear curves of the intraparticle diffusion model that are shown in Fig. S9 are not single lines, which implied that the adsorption of TC molecules on the surface and inner of UiO-67/NSC or UiO-67 involved more than one step.

In detail, TC molecules were transferred to the surface of UiO-67/NSC through boundary layer diffusion from the contaminated solution in the first step [21], and then the TC molecules were combined with the active sites in UiO-67/NSC by intraparticle diffusion from the surface of the adsorbents, which was similar to TC adsorption process of UiO-67. Notice that the linear curves were not through the origin, which indicated that the main rate-limiting step of TC adsorption was not intraparticle diffusion. The intercept of the applied intraparticle diffusion model for UiO-67/NSC was larger than that for UiO-67, which suggested boundary layer diffusion also played an important role in TC adsorption and removal, and the TC diffusion on UiO-67/NSC was faster than that on UiO-67. These results indicated that 0D and 2D structure in UiO-67/NSC is benefit for the improvement of TC adsorption efficiency and capacity.

To investigate the mechanism of TC adsorption further, the isotherm experimental data were simulated with Langmuir and Freundlich isotherm models, and the relative isotherm models are expressed in Eqs. (6) and (7), respectively.

where C e (mg/L) and q e (mg/g) are the concentration of TC and the adsorption capacity of UiO-67/NSC or UiO-67 at adsorption equilibrium, respectively. kL (L/mg) and kF (mg1−1/n L1/n g−1) are equal to the Langmuir constant and Freundlich constant, respectively. q max (mg/g) is the theoretical maximum adsorption capacity for TC on UiO-67/NSC or UiO-67. 1/n is related to adsorption intensity between TC molecules and adsorbents (Fig. 8 , Fig. S10).

Compared with the Freundlich isotherm, the Langmuir isotherm exhibited a great fitness to the experimental data with a high R2 (>0.99) (Table S3), which indicated that TC molecules were adsorbed by the specific sites on the homogeneous surface of UiO-67/NSC [36], and chemisorption occurred in the TC adsorption. In addition, the parameter of 1/n was<1, revealing UiO-67/NSC had favorable adsorption behavior and great affinity towards TC molecules. The maximum adsorption capacity of UiO-67/NSC for TC (q max) was 427.35 mg/g, and this was comparable and even higher than most adsorbents including bulk UiO-67 (Table S4), resulting from the lower diffusion barrier and enhanced mass transfer rate of 2D structure in adsorbents. These results indicated that the prepared UiO-67/NSC with 2D sheets structure has great potential for effective adsorption and removal of TC residues and for making up for defects of 0D NSC and bulk UiO-67.

Regeneration ability of the adsorbents play a vital role in reducing the cost. As shown in Fig. S11, the adsorption capacity of TC on UiO-67/NSC was gradually decreased with the increase of adsorption–desorption cycles. After five cycles, the adsorption capacity was decreased to 257.45 mg/g, which also can meet the requirement of water purification. Compared with the first adsorption cycle (100%), the removal efficiency of following four cycles were higher than 80%, respectively, suggesting the prepared adsorbent is reusable for the adsorption of TC residue.

The mechanism of UiO-67/NSC for recognizing and adsorbing TC molecules was investigated by FTIR, XPS, and Zeta potential analyses. The characteristic peaks of UiO-67/NSC (ZrO bonds, 769 cm−1 and 662 cm−1) were shifted toward lower wavenumbers after adsorption of TC molecules, which indicated that TC molecules had been bound by the functional groups on the surface of UiO-67/NSC (Fig. S12). In detail, the ZrO cluster in UiO-67/NSC that acted as Lewis acid active sites reacted with weak alkaline sites of TC molecules such as NH2 groups, which is a chemical reaction process consistent with adsorption kinetics and isotherms [37]. The stretching of OH/NH groups (3420 cm−1) shifted to 3412 cm−1 in the spectrum for UiO-67/NSC-TC, which implied that the hydrogen bonding was formed by hydroxyl, carboxyl and amino groups of UiO-67/NSC and TC molecules [38]. Moreover, the CO (1672 cm−1), CC (1620 cm−1) OH (1356 cm−1), and CN (1190 cm−1 and 1038 cm−1) stretching of TC molecules were displayed in the spectrum for UiO-67/NSC-TC [39,40]. The OH stretching was diminished to 1296 cm−1, which further revealed that TC molecules were adsorbed on UiO-67/NSC through hydrogen bonding. The peak at 1461 cm−1 was assigned to benzene ring stretching in TC molecules, and its stretching intensity decreased to 1424 cm−1 in UiO-67/NSC-TC [20], which indicated that the benzene ring of TC molecules had been captured by the aromatic backbone of organic ligands on the surface of UiO-67/NSC through π-π interactions.

To clarify the reaction between UiO-67/NSC and TC residue, XPS spectra were employed (Fig. 4, Fig. 9 ). The characteristic elemental peaks of UiO-67/NSC, which included C1s, O1s, N1s, and Zr3d, emerged in the survey spectrum for UiO-67/NSC after adsorption of TC (UiO-67/NSC-TC) (Fig. 4a) [41]. However, the intensity of S2p peaks decreased slightly after adsorption of TC, which resulted from the proportion of sulphur that decreased on the surface of TC adsorbed to UiO-67/NSC. The peaks of O1s for UiO-67/NSC before and after adsorption of TC are presented in Fig. 9a and Fig. 9b, respectively. The peaks of ZrO (530.5 eV), CO (531.6 eV), and CO (532.7 eV) occurred in both spectra of UiO-67/NSC and UiO-67/NSC-TC, which suggested that the main support structure of UiO-67/NSC persisted after adsorption of TC residue [42]. In particular, the OH peak at 530.8 eV in the TC functional group emerged in UiO-67/NSC-TC and the area rate of ZrO peak decreased from 42.22% to 11.98%, indicating that the hydrogen bond interaction occurred in the process of UiO-67/NSC adsorbing TC molecules, [43,44] which was consistent with the FTIR analysis. Furthermore, the C1s spectra of UiO-67/NSC before and after TC adsorption are presented in Fig. S13. Compared with C1s spectra of UiO-67/NSC, UiO-67/NSC-TC had the same peaks CS (283.9 eV), CC (284.4 eV), CC (285.4 eV), CN/CO (286.0 eV), CO (288.3 eV), and π-π (290.4 eV) [24], but their content changed significantly by adsorbing TC (Table S5), which implied that CO and π-π interactions occurred in the process of adsorbing TC. These results demonstrated further that prepared adsorbents had a great affinity for TC molecules.

To investigate the electrostatic interaction between TC molecules and UiO-67/NSC, Zeta potential analysis was conducted (Fig. S14). The point of zero charge of UiO-67/NSC occurred at pH 2.85. The positive charges on the surface of UiO-67/NSC decreased from pH 2 to 2.85, and then negative charges accumulated with the increase in media pH from 2.85 to 10. The higher adsorption capacity of TC on UiO-67/NSC was observed at an initial pH of 3.0 (Fig. S8), which resulted from the strong electrostatic attraction between TCH2 ± (pH < 3.3) and electronegative UiO-67/NSC (pH > 2.6), an acid-base reaction, and hydrogen bonding. Additionally, the combination of TC molecules promoted a charge transfer between the multi-structural adsorbent and the targets that simultaneously quenched the fluorescence of the UiO-67/NSC [26,45], which was favorable for the TC adsorption process.

The possible TC recognition mechanism was also studied by UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of TC molecules had two typical characteristic absorption peaks from 200 to 400 nm, and the TC adsorption peaks overlapped the excitation spectrum of the UiO-67/NSC smart adsorbent (Fig. S15), which suggested that the inner filter effect (IFE) participated in the fluorescence quenching process [46]. In addition, emission spectrum of UiO-67/NSC was also covered partially by the UV–vis adsorption spectrum of TC molecules, which implied that UiO-67/NSC was an energy donor that transferred energy to TC molecules (fluorescence resonance energy transfer) that led to the quenching of the UiO-67/NSC fluorescence [47]. These results indicated that both IFE and fluorescence resonance energy transfer played important roles in the fluorescence quenching for TC identification. The corresponding possible adsorption mechanisms are schematically illustrated in Scheme 1.

The proposed UiO-67/NSC adsorbent for recognition of TC in real samples was investigated (Table S6). The pretreatment of water, fish muscle, and sheep muscle samples were based on the previous reported method with some modification (shown in Supplemental Information) [48,49]. The experimental samples have no obvious influence on emission spectrum or fluorescence intensity of the TC detection system, which implied that UiO-67/NSC could be used to detect TC residue in real samples. Moreover, satisfactory recoveries of the UiO-67/NSC smart adsorbent for detecting TC with different spiked concentrations (0.5–20 mg/L) were obtained in the range of 87.12%–107.52%, which was calculated by Eq. (8).
(8)
R =
C d
C s
x 100
where R (%) is the recovery of the established TC sensing method. Cd (mg/L) is the detected concentration of target molecules, and Cs (mg/L) is the spiked concentration of TC. The relative standard deviation (RSD) of TC sensing systems was < 6.03% for all samples, which further verified that the UiO-67/NSC-based system was reliable for sensing TC in real samples [50], and UiO-67/NSC has the great potential in selective adsorption and removal of TC residual.

Two human cell types were used to assess the cytotoxic effects of UiO-67/NSC on humans. As shown in Fig. S16, there was not significant apoptosis in HepG2 and Caco-2 cell lines in the presence of UiO-67/NSC (20 mg/L), and cell viabilities were > 94.9%, which indicated that UiO-67/NSC can be used in detection and removal of antibiotic residues without cytotoxic effects. Moreover, with a further increase in the concentration of the UiO-67/NSC smart adsorbent (50–100 mg/L), cell viabilities increased to about 100%, which further suggested there were low cytotoxic effects of the UiO-67/NSC on human cells. In particular, the higher concentration of UiO-67/NSC produced lower toxicity than the concentration that we used in adsorption and detection, which may result from a decrease in specific surface area and active sites on the surface of UiO-67/NSC because of the aggregation between adsorbents [51]. These results verified that a concentration of 20 mg/L of UiO-67/NSC was safe for human cells and also acceptable to recognize and to remove TC residue for contaminant control.

A 3D Zr-MOF-based multi-structural adsorbent, UiO-67/NSC, was fabricated by introducing NSC as heterogeneous nucleation and growing UiO-67 up to nanoflower. The synthesized UiO-67/NSC performed well in both sensing and adsorption of TC. In detail, UiO-67/NSC could efficiently recognize the TC with a lower detection limit (0.063 mg/L) and worked very well in practical application with high recoveries (87.12% − 107.52%). Particularly, TC adsorption capacity of bulk UiO-67 was significantly enhanced to 427.35 mg/g under the optimized conditions by converting it into 2D nanosheet structure with low mass transfer barrier and great affinity toward TC. Promoting of adsorption capacity also relied on hydrogen bonding, Lewis acid-base reaction, and π-π interactions between TC and active sites of UiO-67/NSC. Cytotoxic tests demonstrated that UiO-67/NSC with 0D carbon dots and 2D nanosheets was non-toxic and environmental-friendly. These results indicated changing bulk UiO-67 to the flower-on-sheet 3D hierarchical UiO-67/NSC is benefit for sensitive and effective removal of TC residue, and UiO-67/NSC has great potential in treating of other antibiotics residue from surround environment without secondary pollutions. This approach presents a novel strategy for designing multi-functional and green MOF-based adsorbents for the purification of contaminated water.
